summary: TryGetValidatableProperty's DeclaredOnly->FlattenHierarchy fallback still throws AmbiguousMatchException when a property is `new`-hidden at an INTERMEDIATE ancestor and the leaf/runtime model type does not redeclare it (FlattenHierarchy does not flatten instance members) — the fix is incomplete for multi-level hiding.

No test exercises the still-throwing intermediate-hiding fallback path: ValidatesPropertyHiddenAtMultipleInheritanceLevels uses DeepDerivedModel which redeclares Tag (so DeclaredOnly succeeds), and MidLevelModelWithShadow is never instantiated as the leaf under test, so the dangerous FlattenHierarchy-ambiguity path has zero coverage.

Sibling call site DefaultClientValidationService.BuildMetadata resolves a validatable property with the same plain GetProperty(name) pattern and is left unfixed by this PR, so client-side validation still throws AmbiguousMatchException on the same hidden-member models. (This file is NOT part of the PR diff.)

The BindingFlags.FlattenHierarchy flag on the fallback GetProperty call is inert/no-op for an instance-only lookup (it only affects static-member surfacing) and is misleading — it suggests hierarchy-safe traversal it does not provide, and is inconsistent with the repo's other FlattenHierarchy usages.

The two-step DeclaredOnly->FlattenHierarchy lookup has no rationale comment explaining the AmbiguousMatchException it works around, so a future maintainer could 'simplify' it back to a single GetProperty call and silently reintroduce the bug.

Both new GetProperty calls omit BindingFlags.Static (present in the original default flags), so a public static property matching a field name is now silently no longer validated — an undocumented behavior change (locked in only by the IgnoresStaticProperty test).
The PR description claims a try/catch(AmbiguousMatchException)-with-retry fix, but the merged code contains no try/catch (it uses BindingFlags instead) — stale/divergent narrative vs. implementation.

The change adds a third property-resolution policy inconsistent with the codebase's existing hidden-member-aware helpers (e.g. MemberAssignment.GetPropertiesIncludingInherited's hierarchy walk); an existing helper could have been reused instead.

When both lookups return null the property is cached as null and validation is silently skipped with no diagnostic/log, and the reflection lookups have no error handling (pre-existing behavior, not introduced by this PR).
The '// If we can't find it, cache null' comment now sits ~13 lines above the actual null-cache write (an unrelated conditional block was inserted between), weakening comment-to-code mapping.

The new lookups are case-sensitive (no BindingFlags.IgnoreCase) and this is untested — a wrong-case field name silently resolves to null and is not validated, despite MatchesPropertyByExactName's name.

Several exotic reflection shapes are untested: protected/private shadowing of a public base member, constructed-generic-base shadowing, null FieldName, indexers/explicit interface implementations, static/instance name collisions.

ValidatesHiddenPropertiesWithPropertyCaching does not actually verify caching — it asserts only validation messages, which are identical whether the PropertyInfo is cached or re-resolved, so it would pass with or without the _propertyInfoCache.

MatchesPropertyByExactName is redundant/misnamed — it duplicates ValidatesHiddenPropertiesWithoutAmbiguousMatchException (same model/field/assertion) and verifies nothing distinct about exact-name matching; tests 1/2/3 overlap heavily.

Weak Assert.Empty-only assertions in SkipsValidationWhenDerivedShadowHasNoAttributes and IgnoresStaticProperty cannot distinguish 'property correctly resolved with nothing to fail' from 'property never found at all' — vacuous-pass risk (no positive control).
IgnoresStaticProperty is misnamed/weak — it frames an incidental side effect (the dropped BindingFlags.Static) as intended behavior, and the instance Value=0 field is misleading noise.
IgnoresStaticProperty is tautological: Assert.Empty(GetValidationMessages()) supposedly passes under BOTH the fixed code and the reverted pre-fix code, so it allegedly proves nothing about the fix.

Inert fixture members set but never read/asserted — ModelWithStaticProperty.Value and DerivedModelWithInheritedOnly.Description are assigned but never validated, and MidLevelModelWithShadow.Tag is never exercised as its own runtime type.
Three of four new test-model hierarchies declare the derived class before its base class, contrary to the base-then-derived convention used by sibling fixtures.
New attributed test-model properties split the validation attribute and the property declaration across two lines, unlike every pre-existing attributed property in the file (single-line).
New leaf test-model classes are declared as bare `class` (implicit private) rather than `private sealed class` like recently-added sibling fixtures (AsyncTestModel/AsyncThrowingModel).
MatchesPropertyByExactName hardcodes the literal "OrderID" instead of nameof(...), diverging from every other reference to that property in the file.
Fixture names ModelWithNamedBase and ModelWithBaseName are near-anagrams for two unrelated scenarios (a mix-up hazard); some fixture names (DeepDerivedModel) don't convey what they hide.

The static _propertyInfoCache is never reset between tests, so only the first of the three tests keying on (DerivedModelWithHiddenProperty, OrderID) actually exercises the reflection path; tests are not isolated and could become order-dependent.
ModelWithHiddenBaseProperty has low cohesion — it serves two unrelated scenario families (the OrderID two-level hide and the Tag three-level hide) on one base type.

The load-bearing base members typed `object` (the object-vs-int mismatch that creates the distinct shadowed reflection member driving the repro) and the multi-level Tag shadow intent are undocumented; a maintainer could 'align' the types and silently break the repro.
All new tests construct `new FieldIdentifier(model, nameof(...))` directly instead of the sibling-canonical `editContext.Field(nameof(...))` helper.
No test proves that the most-derived attributes win when BOTH a base and a derived shadow carry validation attributes (only the base-dropped / unattributed-shadow direction is covered).
In ValidatesHiddenPropertiesWithoutAmbiguousMatchException the first NotifyFieldChanged fires with no intervening assertion before the state changes again — a confusing/dead call.
The orderIdIdentifier local variable's casing diverges from the file's existing variable-casing convention.

The structural invariant that each fixture actually contains shadowing/ambiguity is unguarded — a future edit dropping `new` or the base property would silently neuter a test; no guard asserts the fallback lookup genuinely throws.
The cache invalidation / hot-reload path (ClearCache, MetadataUpdater.IsSupported gating, negative-result caching) is entirely untested.

ValidatesInheritedPropertyFromBaseClass does not exercise hiding/ambiguity (BaseName is declared once, never shadowed) so it passes on pre-fix code too — it guards the step-2 fallback for plain inheritance, not the ambiguity fix.
Fixtures mix 'Hidden', 'Shadow', and plain 'new' vocabulary for the same concept, and DeepDerivedModel is the only leaf name that doesn't say what it hides — inconsistent naming.
New test model classes encode subtle reflection scenarios (object-vs-int shadowing, static exclusion, three-level shadow) via class shape alone, with no explanatory comments.
Assert.False(editContext.Validate()) in a hidden-property test exercises the whole-form Validator.TryValidateObject path rather than the TryGetValidatableProperty two-step lookup under test.
